title: When one item has items of its own
checkLabels.t2: There is a list inside an item
checkLabels.t4: The outer list has three items
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the list instead of organising it. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: The inner <ul> is INSIDE an <li>. So it closes before that <li> does.
hint3.not-closed:
Whatever you opened last, you close first.
  • →
      →
    • →
      • →
    →
    • .
hint4.not-closed:
Check: every
  • has a
    • , every
      has a
    . And that the inner comes before the of the tyres.
why.still-flat: There is no list inside an item. The front and rear tyre still sit level with the frame and the bell — as if they were separate parts of the bike. They are not. They are part OF the tyres.
hint2.still-flat: You are not moving the items around. You are putting a new list inside one of them.
hint3.still-flat:
• Tyres
    • Front
    • — the new
      is the content of "Tyres", not its neighbour.
hint4.still-flat:
Inside
  • Tyres — after the word, before you close the
    • — open a whole new
      and put both tyres in it.
why.no-tyres: "Tyres" is gone. The task is to put the front and rear INSIDE it, not to delete it.
hint2.no-tyres: The word Tyres must stay. It is the thing the other two belong to.
why.wrong-outer: The outer list does not have three items. There should be exactly three: frame, tyres, bell. If there are five — the tyres are still outside. If there are four — you most likely closed the before the inner list, and it popped out.
hint2.wrong-outer: Count the top-level bullets in the result. There should be three.
hint3.wrong-outer:
Here is the usual mistake:
  • Tyres
      • … — you close the tyres and only then open the list. It looks almost the same, but the list is no longer inside anything.
hint4.wrong-outer:
The closing of the tyres goes AFTER the inner , not before the opening
    .
why.lost-inner: The inner list is not complete. Both the front and the rear tyre must be in it.
hint2.lost-inner: Two tyres — two <li> inside the inner <ul>.
why.empty-li: There is an empty item in the list. Either put text in it or remove it.
blocks.0.text: You are listing what the price includes. There are two tyres — front and rear. You wrote them as new items. Run it and look.
blocks.1.text: The list comes out with five bullets, all equal. It claims that "Front" is the same kind of thing as "Frame" and "Bell" — a separate part of the bike. It is not. It is part OF the tyres.
blocks.2.text: A list is not a drawing. It makes a claim: these things are of the same kind.
blocks.4.text:
Inside a single
  • you can put a whole new
      . Not after it, not next to it — INSIDE, before you close the . That way the new list is not a neighbour of "Tyres", it is its content.
blocks.7.text: Look at the indent and the different bullet. You did not make those — the browser shows on its own that the inner list is on another level. All you said was WHAT belongs to WHAT.
blocks.9.text:
This is the usual mistake: closing right after "Tyres" and only then opening the inner
    . It looks almost the same, but it is not — the list is no longer inside anything. The closing goes AFTER the inner list.
blocks.10.text: You can nest an <ol> inside a <ul> and the other way round — nesting does not care about the type. And you can go deeper still, if it makes sense. Usually it does not.
blocks.11.text: Your turn. Make the front and rear tyre belong to "Tyres".
